comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the Pentagon's new restrictions on journalists following a court ruling, with some commenters critiquing media reporting practices and others briefly acknowledging the New York Times' stance. The conversation includes a mix of sarcastic and supportive remarks about news coverage and military transparency.

comments.author: mpalmer
comments.text: I hadn't realized the Times isn't going along with the DoD "de-brand". On this occasion, I will hand it to them.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately notes the New York Times' choice to resist the Pentagon's branding restrictions, reflecting an informed perspective on media independence.

comments.author: _doctor_love
comments.text:
Not sure why people haven't figured it out - bad news is only happening if it is reported. So if you could simply stop reporting the bad news, then they wouldn't be happening. Seems pretty obvious to me.

It's similar to testing in software development. The more tests you have, the more the tests can break. Therefore the ideal number of tests is zero - no tests, no red builds.

Not sure why people can't get with the program here.
comments.analysis: This comment employs flawed logic by suggesting that stopping reporting bad news would stop the events themselves, which is inaccurate and dismissive of journalistic importance.

comments.author: nkurz
comments.analysis: This comment provides only a link without context, offering no substantive contribution to the discussion.
